citation.title: Electro-osmotic membrane dewatering: Influence of membrane morphology, surface charge and process parameters

content: A B S T R A C T Membrane dewatering is a sustainable way to concentrate biomass without the phase-change penalty of thermal dewatering. Pressure driven dewatering induces fouling, severely reducing flux, and is unsuitable for shearand pressure-sensitive species. Electro-osmotically (EO) driven membrane dewatering circumvents this, but the role of membrane properties is hardly explored. This study investigates how membrane morphology and properties of commercial polyethersulphone (PES) membranes govern EO transport and dewatering performance using an electrical potential as driving force. Higher permeability is observed for feed solutions inducing the highest zeta potential and hydrophilicity at the membrane-liquid interface. As membrane pore radius and surface-to-volume ratio are interconnected, EO per­formance depends on a balance between both. The pore radius must be larger than the Debye length in the pores, but too large pores result in reduced S/V ratios thus inducing weaker interfacial effects. EO membrane dew­atering performance of feeds with dispersed solids (Na-CMC; model biomass) shows the presence of solids in the feed enhances EO flow due to the significantly increased solid-liquid interfacial area available for water trans­port. Depending on its properties, the membrane introduces additional transport resistance, emphasizing the importance of proper membrane selection.

citation.title: Electro-osmotic membrane dewatering: Influence of membrane morphology, surface charge and process parameters

content: s­port. Depending on its properties, the membrane introduces additional transport resistance, emphasizing the importance of proper membrane selection. When experimentally comparing pressure and electrically driven membrane dewatering, pressure driven dewatering shows severe flux decline with increasing biomass concen­tration due to membrane fouling. Conversely EO permeation accelerates with increasing biomass concentration and much higher concentration factors are obtained. This gives EO-based membrane dewatering a significant advantage over pressure driven processes for the dewatering of biomass-feeds or shearand pressure-sensitive species.

content: 1. Introduction Dewatering is a unit operation commonly employed in the process industry to remove water from a feed solution, typically to concentrate suspended or dissolved solids [1–6]. Of the available dewatering tech­nologies [1,5–8], membrane-based dewatering processes are widely used due to their ability to concentrate products at a molecular level without the high energy penalty of phase transitions [3,9,10]. To make this concrete: A recent study comparing the concentration of potato fruit juice using thermal drying only or a hybrid process combining nano­filtration followed by thermal removal (i.e. evaporation) of the remaining water showed that an initial volume reduction of 53 % using nanofiltration reduces the total energy consumption of the process by 53 % as well [11]. In industrial plants producing dried starch, raising feed concentrations before any thermal step, resulted in 41–46 % reductions in steam demand [12]. Similarly in microalgae utilization studies, typical dryer energy demands are large (e.g., spray drying is roughly 55 kWh per kg dried biomass) [13]. Menon et al. showed that in several cases both energy demand as well as greenhouse gas footprint in an electrically driven electro osmotic dewatering process were both about an order of magnitude lower when compared to thermal dew­atering [14]. Many more examples of processes where membrane-based dew­atering is implemented can be found in e.g.

content: [20,27] or concentration [2,10,19]. However, a high solid content in the feed causes severe concentration polarization [28,29] and membrane fouling [20,30] leading to a reduced permeability, lower recoveries, and often a lower product quality. Applying an external electric field as an alternative driving force can overcome these limitations: it enhances mass transport, reduces permeation resistance, and improves separation efficiency [23,25,26,31–35]. Furthermore, electrification of dewatering processes allows direct utilization of solar and wind energy, both sustainable sources, reducing greenhouse gas emissions and avoiding any transmission or conversion losses. Most importantly though, is that it avoids energy intensive water evaporation as electrically driven drying methods do not involve a phase transition, and water removal utilizes the movement of charge (ions). The enhancement in water transport under an applied electric field is governed by the phenomenon of electro-osmosis (EO) [14,36–39]. When an electric field is applied across a charged solid–liquid interface, the diffusive region of the electric double layer (EDL) migrates along the field lines. This migration induces a slip to the water layers adjacent to the charged surface, generating an EO flow (Fig. 1). In the context of industrial feeds, such solid-liquid interfaces are present at the membrane surface and around solids in a feed, enabling EO flow to occur across these charged surfaces.

content: such solid-liquid interfaces are present at the membrane surface and around solids in a feed, enabling EO flow to occur across these charged surfaces. Since EO flow is induced by electromigration of the EDL, its prop­erties significantly impact the overall EO flow [37,38]. The EDL is composed of counterions from the aqueous medium that are attracted to the charged solid surface, which exerts an electric potential ϕo. The electric potential (Equation (1)) is commonly represented by the zeta potential ζ, which is often used as a figure of merit for EDL formation. ϕ(y) = ϕo⋅e -yλD and ϕ(y) = ζ at y = λD(1) Where ϕ is the electric potential (V) calculated at distance y (m) from the charged surface, ζ is the zeta potential (V) and λD is the Debye length (m), a decay constant for the electric potential. λD is calculated as (Equation (2)): λD = ̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅ε⋅kB⋅T2⋅e2⋅I √

content: Where ε is the electromagnetic permittivity (F/m) of the aqueous environment, kB is the Boltzmann constant (J/K), T is the temperature (K), e is the electronic charge (C), and I is the ionic strength of the surrounding solution (M). Following Equation (2), the ionic strength of the surrounding solu­tion inversely impacts the Debye length and the availability of ions in the aqueous medium is thus essential in EDL formation. A higher con­centration of ions in the feed results in the formation of an electrically and structurally denser EDL. This denser EDL can drag adjacent water layers more effectively thus enhancing the achieved EO flow [37]. The use of an electric field as a superimposed [25,26,31,32,35] or stand-alone [23,40,41] driving force has been reported to increase dewatering performance, attributed to the relativistic EO flow of water across charged surfaces. However, current studies that investigate EO driven membrane-based dewatering, still neglect the contribution of membrane properties on the overall dewatering performance, while this information is essential to implement EO driven membrane-based dewatering systems. On a neutral membrane e.g. polyethersulphone (PES), surface charges develop due to acid-base interactions between the components in solution and the functional groups on the membrane, as shown in Fig. 2 [42].

citation.title: Electro-osmotic membrane dewatering: Influence of membrane morphology, surface charge and process parameters

content: e charges develop due to acid-base interactions between the components in solution and the functional groups on the membrane, as shown in Fig. 2 [42]. This leads to EDL formation along the PES membrane-liquid inter­face, both at the surface and in the pores, with the mobile ions in the diffusive layer migrating along the applied electric field lines, inducing EO flow in the direction of the membrane pores (Fig. 3). The generated EO flow results in permeation of the aqueous medium from the feed side to the permeate side, successfully dewatering the feed solution. Analogous to EDL formation in membrane pores, EDL formation and the associated EO flow can also occur across dispersed micro and nanoscale solids (e.g. biomass) in the feed solution [14,25,36,39,43], as shown in Fig. 4. Unlike pressure driven permeation, EO permeation is less sensitive to membrane fouling and more suitable for shearand pressure-sensitive species as it only allows the relative movement of water without pushing the dispersed solids towards the membrane surface [31,32]. Electric field assisted membrane-based dewatering research shows that applying an electric field improves permeate flux and reduces fouling. For dispersed proteins and microbial feeds, superimposing an electric field over a pressure driven membrane-based dewatering system led to significant reduction in hydraulic resistance and increased permeation [26,32,34].

citation.title: Electro-osmotic membrane dewatering: Influence of membrane morphology, surface charge and process parameters

content: ield over a pressure driven membrane-based dewatering system led to significant reduction in hydraulic resistance and increased permeation [26,32,34]. Furthermore, superimposing an electric field over a forward-osmosis process for dewatering of microalgae mitigated concentration polarization and fouling [33]. EO dewatering was also highly effective for feeds comprising of dispersed cellulose [23–25]. The enhanced permeation was attributed to EO flow across the cellulose surface and the electrophoretic movement of cellulose in the opposite direction [23–25]. Cellulose fibres with a high degree of fibrillation enhance EO flow even more due to the higher charged surface area available [24]. Membrane modification with conductive carbon nano­tubes to increase the surface charge density also enhances EO flow [31]. Attempts to quantify the effect of membrane morphology and physical properties on EO flow are limited. Earlier work was performed for nucleopore membranes [44], with a well-defined cylindrical pore geometry, leading to a predictable flow. The study concluded that EO flow decreases in both very small pores, due to EDL overlap [45] and very large ones, due to reduced surface influence. However, the typical nucleopore membrane morphology [44], does not extend to most asymmetric commercial membranes prepared via phase separation that have complex interconnected pores, variable pore sizes and variable interfacial properties. Such systematic studies are lacking. Furthermore,

Membrane contact angle Membrane contact angle measurements were conducted with the aqueous solutions of different pH (Table 2), to evaluate the interfacial interactions and their corresponding role in EO flow. The membrane under investigation was placed in a ring insert with an inner diameter of 47 mm. The subsequent concentration factor is calculated as the ratio of the initial feed volume and the final feed volume. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Membrane surface charge, hydrophilicity and solution characteristics The zeta potential is an indirect way to quantify EDL formation at the membrane-aqueous interface [50]. It yields information about charge formation on a surface in contact with an aqueous medium of a certain pH. Higher zeta potential values can be interpreted as a well-developed EDL, enhancing EO flow. Like the zeta potential, the contact angle is an effective method to analyse (hydrophilic or hydrophobic) interfacial interactions at the solid-liquid interface. Such interactions occur due to the presence of surface charges on the membrane and the counterion in the aqueous medium [50]. A lower contact angle means a more hydrophilic surface that induces spreading and wetting of an aqueous medium over the membrane surface.

citation.title: Electro-osmotic membrane dewatering: Influence of membrane morphology, surface charge and process parameters

content: medium [50]. A lower contact angle means a more hydrophilic surface that induces spreading and wetting of an aqueous medium over the membrane surface. Since EO flow is driven by the movement of water layers adjacent to the membrane surface, the spreading and structure of water layers has significant impact on the EO performance [51]. The measured zeta potentials and contact angles for the different PES membranes wetted with aqueous solutions at different pH values are reported in Table 3. Values reported in this table are for the UP150 membrane. Similar values are obtained for the other three membranes and are reported in Tables S1, S2, and S3 in the Supporting Information. The tables show that all membranes have similar zeta potential and contact angle values, despite possible differences in surface porosity and roughness of the different membranes. Obtained zeta potentials are negative, indicating negative surface charges and polarity of the mem­branes for all three pH values. This is attributed to the acid-base re­actions of the sulphone group in the membrane polymer structure (Fig. 2). The increasing trend in the absolute value of the zeta potential with increasing pH is attributed to a higher number of OH-ions present in the aqueous solution at higher pH [50,52]. This leads to increased surface adsorption of OH-on the membrane surface thereby increasing the surface charge formation and thus the zeta potential.

Similarly, the lowest contact angle is observed for the membranes wetted with a pH 10 solution (θ = 66◦), representing more hydrophilic interactions with the membrane compared to the lower pH solutions. The higher charge density on the solid surface (i.e. higher absolute zeta potential value) and of the wetting phase (i.e. ionic strength I = 0.1 mM for pH 10) at these conditions creates polar interactions due to Coulombic attraction, leading to lower (i.e. more hydrophilic) contact angles [51,53]. In case of pH 4 and 7, the contact angle values are relatively similar (θ = 76◦and θ = 82◦, respectively). While a pH 4 solution has a higher ionic strength (I = 0.1 mM), the corresponding absolute membrane zeta potential is low (ζ = -5.1 mV). Oppositely, in the case of a pH 7 solution, the ionic strength is low (I = 0.0001 mM), but the zeta potential is moderately high (ζ = -28.9 mV). This discrepancy between the ionic composition in the aqueous solution and the charge on the membrane surface reduces the polar interactions between the two phases. Membrane morphology To correlate the EO performance to the structural membrane char­acteristics, morphological membrane characterizations with the selected membranes were performed. Fig. 6 shows the cross-sectional SEM im­ages of the different membranes used. As generally known and visible in the SEM images, all membranes have a thin, denser, selective top layer and a porous membrane sub­structure. In addition, all membranes contain a polypropylene backing material (not visible in the SEM images) that sometimes hinders smooth cryogenic breaking of the membrane samples resulting in somewhat irregular cross sections (i.e. UP010 and UP020). Despite that, all mem­branes show a very similar morphology with, next to the top layer a significant amount of macrovoids present in the substructure. Table 4 reports quantitative characterization data of the different membranes as extracted from adsorption-desorption and volume porosity measurements. The experimental adsorption-desorption iso­therms used to extract the specific surface area are provided in Fig. S1 in the Supporting Information. Important to mention here is that the pore radius reported earlier (Table 1) is the pore radius obtained from the MWCO values and thus characterizes the selective part of the pores in the membrane.

The pore radius mentioned below in Table 4 is the average value of the pore radii of the total membrane, as calculated using Equation (6). A high interfacial area is essential to allow maximum EDL formation leading to higher electromigration and corresponding EO flow [38]. The interfacial area is reported as specific surface area. The highest specific surface area (10.3 m2/g) is measured for the UP010 membrane. This membrane also clearly has the smallest average pore radius relative to the other three membranes (to be discussed below). The specific surface area of the other three membranes is rather similar and no distinct trend can be observed. This most probably originates from the porous bulk structure omnipresent in all membranes, generating only small differences. The membrane volume porosity provides a similar perspective with clearly the lowest value obtained for the membrane with the smallest average pore size, while for the other membranes it increases with increasing pore size or MWCO, similar to literature [54], although dif­ferences are small. A high volume porosity implies more water is present in the membrane structure. Although intrinsically beneficial, it may reduce EO flow due to the dissipation of momentum imparted by the EDL slip to a larger volume of water [37].

content: Although intrinsically beneficial, it may reduce EO flow due to the dissipation of momentum imparted by the EDL slip to a larger volume of water [37]. To better interpret the com­bined influence of specific surface area (S) and specific pore volume (V), the S/V ratio is also reported in Table 4. Following the analysis of the specific surface area and specific pore volume data, the S/V ratio of the membrane with the smallest pore size has a distinctively higher S/V ratio than the other three membranes, which show similar S/V ratios. The S/V ratio, while of no significance to pressure or concentration-driven membrane permeation, is an essential property for EO flow in membrane pores. The S/V ratio quantifies the effective charged interfacial area available for the volume of water present in the porous membrane. A higher S/V ratio thus represents more efficient momentum transfer from electromigration of the EDL to the surrounding fluid [37]. Since EO flow occurs through membrane pores everywhere in the membrane (so not only in the selective membrane top layer), the average pore radius is a key parameter for assessing EO performance. Table 4 also shows the average pore radius of the different membranes investigated.

To clarify, the reported values are not representative for the actual pore radius of the selective top layer of the membranes: The average pore radii reported here are average values of all the pores in the total bulk membrane, thus covering the selective but also the support layer. Consequently, the average pore radius in Table 4 is at least an order of magnitude larger than the pore radius of the selective layer as characterized by e.g. the MWCO of a specific membrane (Table 1). Following the discussion above, as the average pore radius follows from twice the ratio of the specific pore volume and the specific surface area, the lowest average pore radius is reported for the UP010 mem­brane, which also has the lowest MWCO value. The highest average pore size is clearly calculated for the only microfiltration membrane in the series (MP050), whereas the other two membranes (UP020 and UP150) have very comparable values that are, due to the large contribution of the substructure, still rather close to that of the MP050 membrane. 3.3. content: Effect of applied potential A negative membrane surface charge (as shown in Table 3) leads to a positively charged EDL at the solid-liquid interface. Upon application of an electric field, the EDL electromigrates parallel to the surface in the direction of the electric field dragging adjacent water layers along leading to EO flow. Fig. 7 shows the generated EO flow expressed as electro-osmotic permeate volume at different applied electric potential values as a function of time for the porous UP150 membrane. As expected, the amount of EO permeate through the membrane increases in time. With increasing electrical potential, also the volume of permeate in time increases due to the higher driving force and with that enhanced electromigration of the EDL [14,38]. Obviously, the highest permeate volume in time is obtained when a potential of 300 V is used. The inclination of the volume-time curves quantifies the EO flowrate. For all three applied potentials, a steeper inclination in the first 15 min is observed, which tapers to a lower value for the remaining duration of the experiment. Table 5 elucidates a loss of K+ ions from the feed and simultaneously a gain of K+ ions in the permeate, confirming the transport of K+ ions from feed to permeate in time. While at 300 V the mass balance fits, for lower voltages there is a significant difference between loss and gain. We assume this originates from adsorption of K+ ions on the charged membrane surface, which do not have sufficient momentum to desorb again due to the lower electrical driving force. To further investigate the transport of ions during EO, for the experiment performed at 300V, starting from a set initial pH value (4, 7 and 10) in feed and permeate, the final pH in feed and permeate was measured (Table 6). Since the membranes are negatively charged, K+ ions from the aqueous feed form the EDL at the membrane interface and are pulled along the electric field lines. The initially observed high inclination in slope for all three applied electric potentials is attributed to the startup phase where electromigration is driven by these K+ ions in the EDL. Continuous migration of K+ ions from feed to permeate leads to K+ ion depletion in the feed.

content: e electromigration is driven by these K+ ions in the EDL. Continuous migration of K+ ions from feed to permeate leads to K+ ion depletion in the feed. Simultaneously, the electrode reaction at the anode produces H+ ions that, under the influence of and electric po­tential, will, once produced, also migrate in the direction of the electric field lines towards the oppositely charged cathode. After depletion of K+ ions, EDL formation is thus taken over and governed by H+ ions. The EDL composition shifting from K+ ions to H+ ions also explains the change in slope visible in Fig. 6. This originates from the difference in EO drag coefficient between K+ and H+: Earlier work reveals that the electro-osmotic drag coefficient depends on the type of cation [55], with H+ ions having a significantly lower EO drag coefficient and conse­quently a lower amount of water being dragged than K+ ions. This excess of H+ ions in the feed after EO is also confirmed by the pH measurements (Table 6) showing that the feed solutions become more acidic. This highlights that EO performance not only depends on the applied electric potential but also on the type of ions available at the interface. A decrease in the local pH of the feed can also reduce the surface charge formation on the membrane and consequently, EO flow. This is further elaborated on in the subsequent section.

Following the earlier characterizations (Table 3), at those conditions the membrane has both the highest absolute surface charge (ζ = -46 mV) and the strongest hydrophilic aqueous-surface interactions (θ = 66◦).

Membrane zeta potential (surface charge) and contact angle (hydrophilicity) are thus critical metrics in selecting membranes for electro-osmotic dewatering, and at given pH and ionic strength, membranes with the highest zeta potential and most hydrophilic character are thus preferred. 3.3.3. Effect of membrane morphology As EDL formation and EO flow occur over charged surfaces, the morphology and structure in the membrane pores play a major role. The average pore radius, specific surface area (S) and volume porosity (V) as reported in Table 4 are tools to quantify these properties. Fig. 9 presents the relationship between the EO permeability (X-axis) and the average pore radius (left Y-axis) and S/V ratio (right X-axis) of the different membranes using an aqueous feed at pH 10 and an electrical potential of 300 V. Fig. 9 clearly elucidates a balance between average pore radius and S/V ratio in terms of EO permeability. For the membranes investigated, a small pore size (UP010) comes with a high S/V ratio, together resulting in a low EO permeability. For EO flow to occur, the ratio of the pore radius relative to the Debye length is critical. EDL overlap, i.e.

content: her resulting in a low EO permeability. For EO flow to occur, the ratio of the pore radius relative to the Debye length is critical. EDL overlap, i.e. the overlap of the Debye length (λD [m]) and the pore radius (r [m]) occurs when the Debye length is comparable to or larger than the channel width. This is defined as the overlap condition (Equation (7)) [56]: rλD ≲1(7) For all membranes in contact with the aqueous solution of an ionic strength of 0.1 mM the EDL has an estimated Debye length of 30 nm (Equation (2)). For the UP010 membrane, this implies that r/λD = 3.6, which thus is close to the EDL overlap regime hindering EO flow. As the pore radius approaches the Debye length, the ions in the pore experience strong coulombic forces and steric exclusion which can reduce the mobility of ions [57]. This reduces electromigration of the EDL and consequently EO flow. The low EO permeability of the UP010 mem­brane is thus dictated by the small pore size. Only when r/λD > 10 effective bulk EO flow can occur [57–59]. The other three membranes all have an r/λD value well above 10 (UP020 =17.7; UP150 = 15.0; MP050 = 24.1), diverging away from the EDL overlap condition and minimizing reduction in ionic mobility. This is reflected in the significantly higher EO permeability for these mem­branes. The membrane with the largest average pore size (MP050) does not have the highest EO flow though: Due to its high average pore size, this membrane has a relatively low S/V ratio.

content: t average pore size (MP050) does not have the highest EO flow though: Due to its high average pore size, this membrane has a relatively low S/V ratio. Due to this, the charged area available for EO flow is relatively low compared to the amount of volume occupied by the aqueous liquid. Furthermore, the electric po­tential exerted by the pore surface is not significant across the entire pore distance, both reducing the corresponding EO flow. For the highest EO flow through the membranes there thus exists a balance between average pore size and S/V ratio, as also well visible in Fig. 9 where the highest EO permeability is obtained for membranes with an intermediate average pore size and S/V ratio, i.e for the UP020 and UP150 membrane. It is important to note here that the ionic strength of the feed and permeate stream at the start of the experiment is indeed 0.10 mM giving a well-defined Debye length of 30 nm. Due to EO flow during the experiment, the ionic strength in the feed decreases, giving a higher Debye length, However, when calculating the Debye length, it is not possible to take into account the course of the experiment or local var­iabilities in ionic strength. If we resort to an ‘average’ ionic strength of the two solutions at the end of the experiment of 0.055 mM (feed: 0.01 mM; permeate 0.10 mM), the corresponding Debye length would be 40 nm. The ionic current represents the movement of ions from feed to permeate, accounting for electromigration of the EDL [14,36].

The conclusions above are indeed confirmed when the current readings recorded for these experiments are considered (Fig. S3 in the Supporting Information): The current-time plots for the four membranes follow the same order, with the UP010 having the lowest current, followed by the MP050 and the UP020 membrane, and the UP150 membrane shows the highest current i.e. the highest ion transport. As a side note, it is relevant to mention here that in regular mem­brane processes with these PES membranes, separation is governed by the selective top layer pores (for example characterized by the MWCO; Table 1). These pores fall within the EDL overlap regime [56,58], and physically EO flow through these pores is thus restricted. As the mem­branes do show EO flow though, it is plausible that this is governed by the larger pores of the support layer and EO flow in the support layer draws water through the selective top layer. EO in the selective layer pores may still occur but is expected to be minor. The results demonstrate that EO performance is dependent on membrane morphology, with enhanced permeability observed for membranes with a high S/V ratio. Ideally this would thus mean a preference for many but small pores.

However, due to the dependency between S/V ratio and pore size, a high S/V ratio is simultaneously counteracted by EDL overlap, occurring in such very small pores. Membrane morphologies balancing these two aspects are thus preferred offering a high charged surface area and consequently EO flow while circumventing the negative effect of EDL overlap. 3.3.4. Effect of dispersed solids in the feed The primary goal of utilizing EO flow is to efficiently dewater and concentrate feeds with dispersed solids. While there are studies that report the influence of feed composition on the overall flow [14,24,32,36,60], the role of the membrane is not considered. Fig. 10 provides a comparative analysis of the EO performance for membranes tested with a pH 10 aqueous feed solution containing 0.1 wt% Na-CMC as model biomass at an applied potential of 200V. At that pH, the experimentally determined zeta potential of the membranes and Na-CMC is similar, i. e.-46 mV. For comparison, also the results for aqueous feeds without biomass at pH 10 and 200V are presented. Obviously, the charged surfaces of the dispersed particles contribute very significantly to the total EO flow. To interpret this, it is important to realize that the surface charge of the Fig. 9. Journal of Membrane Science 737 (2026) 124759 membranes and the dispersed particles is similar, but that the particle surface area available due to the presence of biomass in the feed is much higher than that of the membrane. Considering that EO transport com­prises of a series of steps, i.e. first through the feed containing the dispersed solids (Fig. 4) and then through the charged membrane pores (Fig. 3), the latter stage can have a significant contribution. Ideally, the contribution of the membrane resistance to the total resistance against EO water transport would be 0 %. For the UP150 and UP020, this contribution is indeed only 5 % and 6 %, respectively, due to the balance between pore size and S/V ratio. For the UP010 membrane though, the resistance contribution of the membrane to EO transport is already 14 %, showing the importance of proper membrane selection for EO dew­atering. To maximize the biomass dewatering rate, the membrane with the highest EO permeability should thus be chosen.

content: r membrane selection for EO dew­atering. To maximize the biomass dewatering rate, the membrane with the highest EO permeability should thus be chosen. State-of-art membrane dewatering is usually performed using pres­sure driven membrane processes (e.g. ultrafiltration). To make a first comparison between electrically driven membrane dewatering and a pressure driven counterpart, the experimental electrical dewatering performance at 200V is compared to the pressure driven dewatering performance at 3 bar for the UP150 membrane using a feed solution with a model biomass (Na-CMC) at pH10 and different biomass con­centrations (Fig. 11). In case of pressure driven dewatering, the permeability of water and consequently the dewatering efficiencies drop very significantly with increasing Na-CMC concentration. This is due to biomass involved in­teractions and subsequent fouling of the membrane [30,61], introducing an additional resistance to water transport. Conversely EO permeability increases with increasing concentration of Na-CMC. This is due to the increased charged surface area in the feed relative to the feed volume available for electro-osmotic flow [24]. This much lower deteriorating effect of fouling gives EO based membrane dewatering a significant advantage over pressure driven processes.

To further quantify the effectiveness of EO based dewatering compared to pressure driven water removal, Table 7 reports, amongst others, the concentration factor for both processes and the concentration of solids before and after dew­atering the feed using EO flow using a UP150 membrane at an applied potential of 200 V. Based on these experiments, the specific energy consumption for EO driven dewatering was calculated (Table 8). Depending on the exact biomass concentration, EO has a specific energy consumption of ~230 kWh/m3. This is of similar magnitude as electro-dewatering of e.g. sludge (i.e., ~80 to 400 kWh/m3 [62]). It is about 2 orders of magnitude higher though than that of pressure driven ultrafiltration (i.e., ~0.15 to 2 kWh/m3 [13]). However, EO-driven membrane dewatering can potentially achieve higher concentration factors, reducing reliance on thermal dewatering (with large energy demands of ~700 kWh/m3 [63]), thus leading to overall energy savings. Table 7 indeed shows that much higher volumes of water are removed when an electric potential is used as driving force (e.g. 449 versus 8 mL for an Na-CMC concentration of 0.1 wt%). In the current example a feed pressure of 3 bar was applied, and obviously higher pressures could be chosen to enhance water permeation.

content: of 0.1 wt%). In the current example a feed pressure of 3 bar was applied, and obviously higher pressures could be chosen to enhance water permeation. However, that will also induce a stronger fouling tendency, further decreasing the relative water flux. Obviously, the results represent a model comparison to show the inherent advantage of electrical dewatering compared to pressure driven processes. In practice, there are still multiple parameters that need optimization and fine tuning to allow large scale optimization. For example, as reported, a change in pH was observed and, in some cases, we observed some electro-oxidation of feed at the anode. Also, gas evolution reactions at the electrode surface may occur in practice. These aspects can, in specific cases, have impact on the biomass (e.g. dena­turation [64] or changing biomass properties [65]). To prevent elec­trode degradation, the electrodes used in this study are made of platinised titanium. This material is commonly used in electrochemistry as an inert electrode that does not undergo any degradation [66,67]. Visual examination of the membrane after 6 h of operation revealed no damage to the membrane structure. All electrochemical reactions occur at the electrode interface which is, in our experiments, placed at a dis­tance of 5 mm from the membrane. However, in practice, such aspects need careful consideration and e.g. an optimized process design isolating electrode reactions from the biomass.

Conclusions This study investigates how the morphology and properties of four commercial PES (UP010, UP020, UP150, MP050 (Microdyn Nadir)) membranes determine EO transport and dewatering performance using an electric field as driving force. EO permeation increases with increasing applied potential. A time-dependency was observed and re­veals a two-phase process originating from the type of ions in the EDL: Initially, K+ ions generate a high EO water flow. Upon K+ ion depletion, this is gradually taken over by H+ ions produced by water electrolysis that have a lower electro-osmotic drag coefficient resulting in lower EO water transport. EO water permeability is highest for a pH 10 solution that simulta­neously yields a high absolute zeta potential and high hydrophilicity of the membrane surface. An enhanced permeability is observed for membranes with a high S/V ratio (i.e. many small pores), but this is counteracted by EDL overlap occurring in very small pores. Membrane morphologies balancing these two aspects are thus preferred. Of the membranes used in this study, the UP150 membrane with an average pore size of 449 nm and an S/V ratio of 4.5⋅106 1/m has the highest EO permeability.

Due to the significantly increased solid-liquid interfacial area available for water transport, EO dewatering of feeds containing dispersed solids (Na-CMC) gives significantly higher water permeabilities. Comparison of pressure and electrically driven membrane dewater­ing shows that pressure driven dewatering experiences severe flux decline with increasing biomass concentration due to membrane fouling. EO is accelerated with increasing biomass concentration and much higher concentration factors can be obtained.
